title: Sets
description: Sets are essential for O(1) membership tests, deduplication, and intersection operations — drill these operations so set-based interview solutions come naturally.

sections.title: unordered_set basics
sections.explanation: **unordered_set<T>** stores unique elements with O(1) average insert, lookup, and delete. Use `.insert(val)`, `.count(val)` (returns 0 or 1), and `.erase(val)`. It's your go-to for checking membership in interviews — much faster than searching a vector.

exercise.prompt: Create an unordered_set with values 10, 20, 30. Try inserting 20 again (it should be ignored). Print the size, whether 20 exists, and whether 40 exists.

validation.message: Use unordered_set with size() and count() methods

exercise.hint: s.size() returns 3 since duplicates are ignored. s.count(20) returns 1, s.count(40) returns 0.

sections.title: Insert, count, and erase
sections.explanation: **insert()** returns a pair<iterator, bool> — the bool tells you if insertion succeeded (false means element already existed). **erase(val)** removes an element and returns the number removed (0 or 1). These are useful for tracking seen elements during traversals.

exercise.prompt: Create a set, insert "cat", "dog", "bird". Erase "dog" from the set. Print the size, then check and print whether "dog" still exists (0 or 1) and whether "cat" exists.

validation.message: After erasing dog: size=2, dog count=0, cat count=1
Use .erase() to remove and .count() to check membership

exercise.hint: animals.erase("dog") removes it. Size becomes 2. count("dog") = 0, count("cat") = 1.

sections.title: Set intersection with sorted sets
sections.explanation: To find common elements between two collections, use **set<T>** (sorted) with **set_intersection** from `<algorithm>`. Both inputs must be sorted — set<T> handles that automatically. This is cleaner than nested loops and runs in O(n+m) time.

exercise.prompt: Find the intersection of set {1, 3, 5, 7, 9} and set {2, 3, 5, 8, 9}. Print the common elements space-separated.

validation.message: Common elements of the two sets are 3, 5, and 9
Use set_intersection with back_inserter to collect results

exercise.hint: Use back_inserter(result) as the output iterator for set_intersection.

sections.title: Deduplication with sets
sections.explanation: A common interview task: remove duplicates from a vector while preserving order. Insert each element into an unordered_set — if it wasn't already there, keep it. Alternatively, sort + unique removes duplicates but loses original order.

exercise.prompt: Given the vector {5, 3, 5, 1, 3, 2, 1}, remove duplicates while preserving the original order. Print the deduplicated elements space-separated.

validation.message: Use count to check, insert to track, and push_back to collect unique elements

exercise.hint: seen.count(x) == 0 means x hasn't been seen yet. Insert it into seen and push_back to result.
